title: South Sudan: Protecting livelihoods and preventing famine - Urgent call for assistance (pdf)
summary: The Organization’s current response spans 25 counties across eight states (Central Equatoria, Eastern Equatoria, Jonglei, Northern Bahr el Ghazal, Upper Nile, Unity, Warrap and Western Bahr el Ghazal), with a strategic focus on the locations with the highest levels of vulnerability. FAO requires USD 10 million To assist 1.8 million people Period May–September 2026 Targeted areas Priority counties within all ten states © FAO/Mayak Akuot Context South Sudan is facing one of the most severe food insecurity crises globally.

Since the start of the conflict in the Sudan in April 2023, South Sudan has experienced a massive influx of over 700 000 returnees and refugees.2 More than 70 percent have settled in already vulnerable areas where FAO is providing support, placing additional strain on limited resources and services.

The Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) is scaling up its response to enable vulnerable families to produce and access nutritious food, protect their livelihoods and prevent further hunger and malnutrition.
FAO is operational in two of the six counties where communities are experiencing Catastrophe (IPC Phase 5) (Luakpiny/Nasir and Ulang), supporting 13 333 households in the most critical areas.

full_text:
FAO requires
USD 10 million
To assist
1.8 million people
Period
May–September 2026
Targeted areas
Priority counties 
within all ten states
© FAO/Mayak Akuot
Context 
South Sudan is facing one of the most severe food insecurity crises 
globally. According to the latest Integrated Food Security Phase 
Classification (IPC) analysis, 55 percent of the population is experiencing 
high levels of acute food insecurity (IPC Phase 3 or above), including 
73 000 people in Catastrophe (IPC Phase 5).1 Additionally, four counties – 
Akobo and Nyirol in Jonglei State and Luakpiny/Nasir and Ulang in 
Upper Nile State – face a risk of Famine under a probable worst-case 
scenario, which has a realistic likelihood of occurring.
The crisis is driven by a combination of recurrent shocks and structural 
vulnerabilities. Four consecutive years of severe flooding have destroyed 
crops, livestock and critical infrastructure, significantly undermining 
agricultural production and livelihoods. Concurrently, protracted 
conflict and insecurity continue to restrict humanitarian access, 
displace communities and disrupt local economies.
Since the start of the conflict in the Sudan in April 2023, South Sudan 
has experienced a massive influx of over 700 000 returnees and 
refugees.2 More than 70 percent have settled in already vulnerable 
areas where FAO is providing support, placing additional strain on 
limited resources and services. This is compounded by a deteriorating 
economic situation characterized by currency depreciation, rising 
food prices and disrupted markets, further eroding households’ 
purchasing power.
These shocks are occurring in a context of widespread structural 
poverty and limited livelihood diversification, leaving households with 
minimal coping capacity and highly vulnerable to further deterioration. 
The Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) is 
scaling up its response to enable vulnerable families to produce and 
access nutritious food, protect their livelihoods and prevent further 
hunger and malnutrition. Under the South Sudan: Emergency and 
Resilience Plan, 2026–2028, FAO requires USD 255 million to support 
5.25 million people. Of this amount, an initial USD 10 million is urgently 
needed to deliver time-critical, life-saving agricultural interventions to 
1.8 million people.
South Sudan
Protecting livelihoods 
and preventing famine 
Urgent call for assistance
FAO’s life-saving agriculture response
Activities implemented and funding received so far
FAO, in collaboration with its local partners, is delivering emergency 
livelihood support to the most vulnerable households across the 
country. Under the current funding envelope of approximately 
USD 3.5 million – comprising USD 1.62 million from the South Sudan 
Humanitarian Fund, USD 140 000 from the Government of Japan, 
and approximately USD 1.7 million from savings and carry-over from 
previous projects – FAO is supporting an estimated 103 000 households 
(approximately 564 000 people) countrywide. A further 9 420 households 
in Unity State are being reached thanks to funding from the Governments 
of Norway and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland.
The Organization’s current response spans 25 counties across eight states 
(Central Equatoria, Eastern Equatoria, Jonglei, Northern Bahr el Ghazal, 
Upper Nile, Unity, Warrap and Western Bahr el Ghazal), with a 
strategic focus on the locations with the highest levels of vulnerability. 
FAO is operational in two of the six counties where communities are 
experiencing Catastrophe (IPC Phase 5) (Luakpiny/Nasir and Ulang), 
supporting 13 333 households in the most critical areas. 
Assistance is currently being delivered to the 103 000 households through 
the following interventions:
• Seed fairs (cash/voucher modality): Approximately 
8 000 households are receiving support through seed fairs, enabling 
them to select their preferred seeds from local markets while 
strengthening local food systems.
• Direct input packages: The remaining 95 000 households are 
receiving livelihood support packages comprising crop seeds 
(groundnut, maize and sorghum), vegetable seeds (amaranth, collard, 
okra, onion and tomato), one hand tool per household, and fishing 
kits to diversify their food sources.
• Livestock support: Current interventions also include the vaccination 
and treatment of approximately 200 000 animals, safeguarding the 
livestock assets of agro/pastoral households.
Scaling up to reach 1.8 million people in need
FAO aims to scale up its emergency livelihood response to reach 
vulnerable people in the most food-insecure areas during the critical 
2026 agricultural season. Additional resources would enable FAO to more 
than triple its current reach, providing a comprehensive, multi-sector 
package of crop, fisheries and livestock support to communities at the 
highest risk of Famine. 
Situation analysis
7.8 million people facing 
high levels of acute food insecurity 
(IPC Phase 3 or above) between 
April and July 2026, including 
73 306 people in Catastrophe 
(IPC Phase 5) 1
2.2 million children under 
5 years of age and 1.2 million 
pregnant or breastfeeding women 
suffering from acute malnutrition1
+304 000 people displaced 
by violence in Jonglei State since 
December 20253 
+80% of people live in rural 
areas and rely on agriculture for food 
and income
Urgent call for assistance | South Sudan
The window to act is rapidly 
closing. Unless agricultural 
inputs reach farmers by June, 
the main planting seasons 
will be lost. We know what 
must be done; delaying action 
will only deepen the crisis, 
drive up response costs and 
increase humanitarian needs 
for millions.
The cost of inaction 
Without immediate and scaled-up support, the humanitarian 
consequences will be severe, irreversible within the season, and likely to 
drive a further deterioration in IPC classification across multiple counties. 
FAO warns of the following cascading impacts if funding is not mobilized 
before the June 2026 planting deadline: 
1. Expansion of areas facing Catastrophe (IPC Phase 5) and risk of Famine 
Catastrophe (IPC Phase 5) is currently documented in six counties across 
Upper Nile and Jonglei states, affecting over 73 000 people. However, the 
structural drivers – persistent flooding, conflict, displacement and market 
failures – remain unaddressed across a much wider geographic area. 
Without emergency livelihood interventions during the current planting 
season, the following projections apply:
• Counties currently in Emergency (IPC Phase 4), including Rubkona in 
Unity, Malakal in Upper Nile, and Canal/Pigi, Duk, Ayod and Pibor in 
Jonglei, face a high risk of deteriorating into Catastrophe (IPC Phase 5) 
conditions during the June–September 2026 lean season.
• The 2.49 million people currently in Emergency (IPC Phase 4) are 
one missed harvest away from catastrophe. A failed main season will 
eliminate their primary domestic food source and erode any residual 
coping capacity.
• An estimated additional 500 000–700 000 people could slide into 
Catastrophe (IPC Phase 5) by the second half of 2026 if no emergency 
agricultural support is provided. 
Sector Urgent action Impact Target Cost per household 
(USD)
Cost per sector 
(USD)
Crops

groundnut, cowpea and sesame), vegetable 
seeds (amaranth, collard, eggplant, okra, onion 
and tomato), and one hand tool per household 
Enable food 
production during 
the main planting 
season

Market-based seed support through seed 
fairs (cash and vouchers) in counties with 
functioning markets
Ensure household 
agency and 
stimulate local 
agricultural trade
Fisheries
Provision of nets, hooks, fishing lines and safety 
equipment to fishing households in flood-prone 
areas (including Jonglei, Unity and Upper 
Nile states) 
Sustain critical 
sources of protein 
and income during 
the lean season
50 000 households 40 2 000 000
Livestock
Vaccination and treatment of approximately 
3 million cattle and small ruminants against 
contagious bovine pleuropneumonia, foot 
and-mouth disease, peste des petits ruminants, 
haemorrhagic septicaemia, blackleg, and 
internal and external parasites 
Protect the 
primary livelihood 
asset and food 
source of pastoral 
communities
3 million animals 
belonging to 
50 000 households 
50 2 500 000
Operations Transport, logistics, monitoring and evaluation, 
and staff support
Enable the delivery 
of urgent assistance All areas 5% of total cost of 
intervention 500 000

households – 10 000 000

Source: Authors’ own elaboration.
2. Missed agricultural season with irreversible impacts within a year 
 
Table 2. Breakdown of the projected consequences of not providing 
emergency agricultural support

delimitation of its frontiers or boundaries.
Notes
1 IPC. 2026. South Sudan: IPC Acute food 
insecurity and malnutrition snapshot, 
April–July 2026. Juba. https://www.ipcinfo.
org/fileadmin/user_upload/ipcinfo/docs/
IPC_SouthSudan_Projection_update_
Acute_Food_Insecurity_Malnutrition_April_
July2026_Snapshot.pdf
2 UNHCR (UN Refugee Agency). 2026. Sudan 
situation. In: Operational data portal. [Cited 
15 May 2026]. https://data.unhcr.org/en/
situations/sudansituation
3 OCHA (United Nations Office for the 
Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs). 
2026. South Sudan Humanitarian Update 
18–30 April 2026. Juba. https://www.unocha.
org/publications/report/south-sudan/south-
sudan-humanitarian-update-18-30-april-2026 
© FAO/Shuaib Shamrouk
If we don’t act by 
June 2026 Projected consequences
No crop seeds 
provided
200 000+ households miss the main planting window entirely, 
resulting in no domestic food production for 12 months
No fishing kits 
distributed
50 000+ fishing households lose access to a vital source of protein 
and income during the peak fishing season, and post harvest 
losses increase
No livestock 
vaccination
3 million animals are at risk of preventable disease outbreaks, and 
livestock mortality erodes the primary wealth and food asset of 
pastoral communities
No seed fairs 
conducted
Local seed markets collapse, informal seed networks are 
disrupted, and community resilience is further degraded
No intervention in 
IPC 5 areas
16 484 people in Luakpiny/Nasir County and 7 656 in Ulang 
County face deepening Famine with no domestic food 
production fallback
Source: Authors’ own elaboration.
3. Multiplier effects on agrifood systems and markets 
• Failure to support the current planting season will drive increased 
dependence on food assistance programmes, placing additional 
pressure on already-strained World Food Programme and 
non-governmental organization pipelines.
• Livestock losses from preventable disease outbreaks are irreversible 
within a season and take years to rebuild, permanently reducing 
community resilience and productive capacity.
• Market disruption resulting from livelihood collapse will further inflate 
local food prices, disproportionately affecting the urban poor and 
displaced populations in Juba, Malakal and Renk – cities already 
hosting large IPC 3+ populations.
4. Returnee and refugee pressure
Since April 2023, large numbers of returnees and refugees have entered 
South Sudan from the Sudan, with most settling in counties covered by 
current and proposed FAO interventions, particularly in Renk (Upper Nile 
State), where nearly 377 000 people are already in IPC Phase 3+. This 
sustained influx places extraordinary pressure on host community 
agrifood systems. Without scaled-up support, host communities will 
exhaust their remaining coping mechanisms, triggering inter-community 
tensions and leading to further displacement.
© FAO/Mayak Akuot

related.excerpt: THE GENEV A CONVENTIONS OF 12 AUGUST 1949 THE GENEV A CONVENTIONS OF 12 AUGUST 1949 ICRC 0173/002 05.2010 10,000 Mission The International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) is an impartial, neutral and independent organization whose exclusively humanitarian mission is to protect the lives and dignity of victims of armed conflict and other situations of violence and to provide them with assistance. The ICRC also endeavours to prevent suffering by promoting and strengthening humanitarian law and universal humanitarian principles. Established in 1863, the ICRC is at the origin of the Geneva Conventions and the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement. It directs and coordinates the international activities conducted by the Movement in armed conflicts and other situations of violence. related.title: United Nations Charter
related.summary: CHAPTER II MEMBERSHIP Article 3 The original Members of the United Nations shall be the states which, having participated in the United Nations Conference on International Organization at San Francisco, or having previ- ously signed the Declaration by United Nations of January 1,1942, sign the present Charter and ratify it in accordance with Article 110.
bullets: CHAPTER II MEMBERSHIP Article 3 The original Members of the United Nations shall be the states which, having participated in the United Nations Conference on International Organization at San Francisco, or having previ- ously signed the Declaration by United Nations of January 1,1942, sign the present Charter and ratify it in accordance with Article 110.

Article 5 / A Member of the United Nations against which preventive or enforcement action has been taken by the Security Council may be suspended from the exercise of the rights and privileges of mem- bership by the General Assembly upon the recom- mendation of the Security Council.

Article 6 A Member of the United Nations which has persistently violated the Principles contained in the present Charter may be expelled from the Organization by the General Assembly upon the recommendation of the Security Council.

Accordingly, our respective Governments, through representatives assembled in the city of San Francisco, who have exhibited their full powers found to be in good and due form, have agreed to the present Charter of the United Nations and do hereby establish an international organization to be known as the United Nations.
CHAPTER I PURPOSES AND PRINCIPLES Article 1 The^Purposes of the United Nations are: 1.
related.excerpt: CHARTER OF THE UNITED NATIONS AND STATUTE OF THE INTERNATIONAL COURT OF JUSTICE SAN FRANCISCO • 1945 CHARTER OF THE UNITED NATIONS WE THE PEOPLES OF THE UNITED NATIONS DETERMINED to save succeeding generations from the scourge of war, which twice in our life- time has brought untold sorrow to mankind, and to reaffirm faith in fundamental human rights, in the dignity and worth of the human person, in the equal rights of men and women and of nations large and small, and to establish conditions under which justice and respect for the obligations arising from treaties and other sources of international law can be maintained, and to promote social progress and better standards of life in larger freedom, AND FOR THESE ENDS to practice tolerance and live together in peace with one another as good neighbors, and to unite our strength to maintain international peace and security, and to ensure, by the acceptance of principles and the institution of methods, that armed force shall not be used, save in the common interest, and to employ international machinery for the promotion of the economic and social advancement of all peoples, HAVE RESOLVED TO COMBINE OUR EFFORTS TO ACCOMPLISH THESE AIMS

related.title: UN Peacekeeping Capstone Doctrine
related.summary: 2008 Over the past six decades, United Nations peacekeeping has evolved into a complex, global undertaking. During this time, the conduct of United Nations peacekeeping operations has been guided by a largely unwritten body of principles and informed by the experiences of the many thousands of men and women who have served in the more than 60 operations launched since 1948. United Nations Peacekeeping Operations Principles and Guidelines 2008 Over the past six decades, United Nations peacekeeping has evolved into a complex, global undertaking.

related.title: The World Factbook
related.summary: The World Factbook appealed to researchers, news organizations, teachers, students, and international travelers. Some readers even inquired whether their preferred geographic designation or world entity could be included on the high-profile site. The original classified publication, titled The National Basic Intelligence Factbook, launched in 1962.
bullets: The World Factbook appealed to researchers, news organizations, teachers, students, and international travelers.
Some readers even inquired whether their preferred geographic designation or world entity could be included on the high-profile site.
The original classified publication, titled The National Basic Intelligence Factbook, launched in 1962.
In 1997, The World Factbook went digital and debuted to a worldwide audience on CIA.gov, where it garnered millions of views each year.

Finally, only CIA insiders would know that officers donated some of their personal travel photos to The World Factbook, which hosted more than 5,000 photographs that were copyright-free for anyone to access and use.
related.excerpt: One of CIA’s oldest and most recognizable intelligence publications, The World Factbook, has sunset. The World Factbook served the Intelligence Community and the general public as a longstanding, one-stop basic reference about countries and communities around the globe. Let’s take a quick look into the history of The World Factbook. Over many decades, The World Factbook evolved from a classified to unclassified, hardcopy to electronic product that added new categories, and even new global entities. The original classified publication, titled The National Basic Intelligence Factbook, launched in 1962. The first unclassified companion version was issued in 1971. A decade later it was renamed The World Factbook. In 1997, The World Factbook went digital and debuted to a worldwide audience on CIA.gov, where it garnered millions of views each year. The World Factbook appealed to researchers, news organizations, teachers, students, and international travelers. Some readers even inquired whether their preferred geographic designation or world entity could be included on the high-profile site. Finally, only CIA insiders would know that officers donated some of their personal travel photos t

related.title: Consolidated Sanctions List
related.summary: Initially convicted on 23 May 2014 by the ICC to 12 years in prison for war crimes and crimes against humanity, the ICC Appeals Chamber reduced his sentence and determined that Katanga’s sentence should be completed on 18 January 2016. Arrested by Congolese authorities in October 2005, acquitted by the Court of Appeal in Kisangani, subsequently transferred to the judicial authorities in Kinshasa on new charges of crimes against humanity, war crimes, murder, aggravated assault and battery.
bullets: Initially convicted on 23 May 2014 by the ICC to 12 years in prison for war crimes and crimes against humanity, the ICC Appeals Chamber reduced his sentence and determined that Katanga’s sentence should be completed on 18 January 2016.

As of 25 March 2011, the High Military Court in Kinshasa opened a trial against Kakwavu for war crimes.
In November 2014, convicted by a DRC military court to ten years in prison for rape, murder, and torture.
related.excerpt: 6907993 1 ERIC BADEGE DRC CDi.001 2012-12-31 Male He fled to Rwanda in March 2013 and is still living there as of early 2016. INTERPOL-UN Security Council Special Notice web link:https://www.interpol.int/en/How-we-work/Notices/View-UN-Notices-Individuals YES https://www.interpol.int/en/How-we-work/Notices/View-UN-Notices-Individuals Democratic Republic of the Congo UN List 2016-10-13 Rwanda as of early 2016 EXACT 1971 6907994 1 FRANK KAKOLELE BWAMBALE DRC CDi.002 2005-11-01 Male Left the CNDP in January 2008. As of June 2011, resides in Kinshasa. Since 2010, Kakolele has been involved in activities apparently on behalf of the DRC government’s Programme de Stabilisation et Reconstruction des Zones Sortant des Conflits Armés (STAREC), including participation in a STAREC mission to Goma and Beni in March 2011. DRC authorities arrested him in December 2013 in Beni, North Kivu Province, for allegedly blocking the DDR process. He left the DRC and lived in Kenya for some time, before being called back by the DRC Government to assist them with the situation in the Territory of Beni.
